title: Senior Albanese minister clashes with Pocock over top-secret AI copyright claim
full_text:
Federal politician David Pocock has clashed with a senior Albanese government minister over a whistleblower claim that federal cabinet is considering plans to let AI companies train on Australian copyright material.
Industry and Science Minister Tim Ayres accused Mr Pocock in Parliament of "reckless speculation" for airing the claim, while Mr Pocock said Mr Ayres did not deny or reject the claims.
At the centre of the stoush was Mr Pocock's allegation that the highest level of government is confidentially considering two competing proposals to change copyright policy for AI training.
Mr Pocock said one proposal being seriously considered would create a copyright "carve-out" for AI companies tied to billions of dollars of data centre investment and hundreds of millions of dollars annually for a creative fund.
The other submission would instead expand licensing agreements that would clear the way for AI companies to legally use Australian material to train their proprietary technology.
Mr Pocock told the ABC that a "credible source" had provided detailed information to his office about the high-level plans.
"One cabinet submission would effectively allow AI companies to text and data-mine Australian creatives' work in return for a multi-billion data centre investment and expedited approvals," he said.
The ABC has not independently verified the whistleblower's identity, the existence of the cabinet submissions, or the contents of the claim described by Senator Pocock's office.
Spokespersons for Senator Ayres and Assistant Minister Andrew Charlton both called the claims "Inaccurate".
"The government has ruled out a text and data mining exception. Our position has not changed," both said.
The offices of Senator Ayres, Assistant Minister Andrew Charlton, Attorney-General Michelle Rowland, Home Affairs and Arts Minister Tony Burke were all contacted for comment.
The government has made repeated commitments that copyright laws would not be watered down in any deal with AI companies since it ruled out a carve-out in October last year.
Pocock and Ayres face off over whistleblower copyright claims
In Question Time on Tuesday, Senator Pocock asked Mr Ayres to rule out any change that would allow AI companies to use Australian creatives' work.
"Minister, is it still the case that the government has no plans to allow AI companies to mine Australian creatives' data, either via some form of carve out, an extension to the licensing system or other means?" Mr Pocock asked.
He then asked whether the government was considering competing proposals tied to a creative arts fund, multi-billion-dollar data centre investment and expedited approvals.
Mr Pocock asked whether any element of that claim was correct, and whether Prime Minister Anthony Albanese intended to announce a plan on or around July 15, or if the government could "categorically rule out" contemplating any change.
My Ayres responded saying the federal government would not undermine copyright protections in Australia, and that it was in Australia's interests to "secure as much of the technology stack" as possible.
"It is not this government's view that we should just be a cork bobbing on the ocean of other people's technology, and a customer at the long end of technology supply chains," he said.
Mr Ayres accused Mr Pocock of "reckless speculation" in a radio interview on Monday that also mentioned whistleblower claims.
Later, Mr Pocock returned to speak in the Senate to say Mr Ayres' answer did not rule out the whistleblower's claims.
"The minister accused me of reckless speculation, but, rather curiously, could not simply rule out the speculation," he said.
Creative sector warns of cost to artists
The political stoush follows months of pressure from technology companies and data centre operators, who argue Australia's copyright system makes it harder to invest in local AI infrastructure.
AI systems are widely reported to be built on vast amounts of data, including copyright material that has not been licensed.
A text and data mining exemption would allow certain copying and analysis of that material without negotiating individual licences in the usual way.
A licensing extension would be a different model because it would preserve some form of payment or permission system. Schools, universities and TAFEs have a similar licensing agreement to let them use books, websites, news articles and other content in return without having to individually negotiate with each rights holder.
The practical difference is whether AI companies can use books, songs, journalism, images and other works to train or test systems without dealing directly with creators and rights holders
ARIA chief executive Annabelle Herd told the ABC the creative industries would be gravely concerned if the government was considering reversing its previous rejection of a text and data mining exception.
"The creative and media industries have not been consulted on such a proposal and would vehemently oppose such a radical intervention into a free market that is burgeoning with commercial opportunity for Australian creators," Ms Herd said.
University of Sydney law professor Kimberlee Weatherall said Australian copyright law was out of date, but any fund raised major questions about who would actually be paid.
"If it is about paying copyright owners, the immediate question is: what about human creators?" Professor Weatherall said.
She said there would also be questions about whether creators could refuse to have their work used, including Indigenous creators and groups who may want culturally significant material to be off limits.
UNSW law professor Kathy Bowrey said the existing system was not fit for purpose, but the case for tying copyright change to data centres should be treated sceptically.
"The argument that we need this in order to operate data centres in Australia is also rubbish," Professor Bowrey said.
"What is the work that the exception is supposed to do, as opposed to a licence?" she said.
The copyright dispute has escalated
In October last year, Attorney-General Michelle Rowland declared that the government would not grant a copyright exemption for AI training.
Since then, tech industry figures have continued to argue the existing law makes it too difficult to train AI systems in Australia.
This month, Atlassian co-founder and Tech Council of Australia chair Scott Farquhar told The Australian Financial Review AI Summit that urgent copyright reform was needed to secure AI investment.
Mr Farquhar said AI companies would need to "cut a deal with every single recording artist in the entire world" to comply with Australia copyright law, and this complexity was scaring away investment.
Ms Rowland reiterated her stance in May.
"The Albanese government is committed to supporting Australia's creative sector and has been clear for some time that there are no plans to weaken copyright protections when it comes to AI," she told the ABC.
Australian Broadcasting Corporation
